response: solution The primary error in the erroneous code is likely the failure to handle duplicate elements correctly when the most frequent element appears more than half the array size, causing adjacent equal values. solution A robust fix involves sorting the array and then placing elements from the sorted list into even and odd indices in a specific interleaved manner to separate duplicates. solution Specifically, we can sort the array, then fill the even positions (0, 2, 4...) starting from the median and moving backwards, and fill the odd positions (1, 3, 5...) starting from the median and moving forwards. solution This strategy ensures that if duplicates exist, they are pushed into positions that are not adjacent in the final sequence. solution For the follow-up requirement of O(n) time without sorting, we can use the Quickselect algorithm to find the median element and then partition the array around it before applying the same interleaving logic. solution This approach works seamlessly for both positive and negative integers because it relies only on relative comparisons rather than absolute value ranges. solution The final solution successfully transforms the input array to meet the wiggle sort constraints while avoiding adjacent equal elements whenever possible.
